note: v6: new dedicated ECG demo video becomes THE doctor demonstration (login->patient->ECG->SHAP->save). doc_demo + old ecg walkthrough consolidated into s09.

lines.text: You see NeuraCard — a multimodal medical AI platform for clinical decision support.
The main problem is simple: one patient might need a brain scan, a heart tracing, an ultrasound, and a brain-wave recording — many separate tests.
Each test is read in isolation — different specialists, different machines, different schedules. No one ever sees the whole patient at once.
AI tools do exist for each test type. But today's models only do one thing — built for a single task, and rarely leaving the research lab.
Even worse, when a model gives a result, it cannot explain how it got there — a verdict with no reasoning that no clinician can really trust.
So the big picture never comes together, right when you need to decide. Data stays fragmented — and that means slower, less complete care.
NeuraCard solves this — one platform for every test type, used by two roles that work together: a technician, and a doctor.

First, the technician. After registering, they open the Convert Data tool and load the raw files — DICOM scans and any other raw data the models can't read directly — and the platform standardises and prepares them. With one click, the cleaned data is uploaded to the patient's record, ready for the doctor to analyse.

Now let's see how a doctor uses it — we'll take the ECG as our example. The doctor logs in, opens a patient, and selects the ECG analysis. NeuraCard reads all twelve leads and returns the result — here, atrial fibrillation, with the heart rate and rhythm. One click on SHAP reveals the model's reasoning: exactly which beats drove the decision, so it's never a black box. The doctor reviews it, and saves it to the patient's record. And the very same process works for every other test.
Take Brain MRI — it detects the tumour and classifies its type, and shows you exactly where it looked. The evidence is shown, not hidden.
The Echocardiogram measures the ejection fraction and chamber segmentation, straight from the echo loop, with the ultrasound frames beside it.
And the EEG reviews the brain-wave recording for harmful activity — multi-channel waveforms, key indicators, and a clear summary.
Every result is explainable. Grad-CAM and SHAP show exactly where each model focused, so a doctor always checks the reasoning, not just the answer.
And it brings them together — findings from brain and heart, combined into one clear, readable interpretation.
Every test result then flows into one combined PDF report — imaging, signals, and interpretation, side by side, ready for the doctor to read, sign, and act on.

Looking ahead, the roadmap is ambitious: learned multimodal fusion, to find brain-heart links no single test can show; prospective hospital trials; real-time monitoring at the bedside and on wearables; and full integration with hospital record systems — on the path to a certified clinical tool.
NeuraCard — every signal, together, for faster, more confident care.
